title: Needle-felted blue tit
subtitle: A compact perching blue tit in merino with yellow breast and blue-grey cap
excerpt: Build a small, rounded blue tit in layers of merino roving, capturing the bright blue-grey cap, yellow breast, and white cheek patches. The finished bird is about 5 cm tall.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional needle-felting practice. Small perching birds are a staple British needle-felting project; the layer-building sequence follows standard guild workshop technique.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dry felting method using barbed needles to tangle wool fibres together without heat or water. The barbs catch fibre scales on each stab, locking them into a firm mass.
Wool fibre drawn into a continuous ribbon, with fibres lying roughly parallel. The standard starting material for needle felting.

content.text: The blue tit has five distinct colour zones. Work each zone in thin layers of
to keep each colour crisp and separate. The five zones are: blue-grey cap, white cheeks, yellow breast, olive-green back, and dark eye stripe.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees onto the foam mat only. Wear a finger guard on the supporting hand.

items.name: Merino roving, dark navy (eye stripe, bib)

content.text: Roll about 8 g of mixed roving into a rounded oval about 4 cm long. Stab it firmly with the size-38 needle, rotating every few passes to maintain an even egg shape. Felt until firm with no loose surface fibres. This core does not need to be any specific colour as every surface will be covered in subsequent layers.
Step 2: Apply the main colour zones

Divide the body into three zones: lower front (yellow breast), upper back (olive green), and head (blue-grey). Tease thin layers of each colour and lay them over the relevant zones, overlapping slightly at the boundaries. Stab each layer in with the size-40 needle. Build colour gradually with two or three thin layers rather than one thick one.

Apply white cheek patches on each side of the head. Add the dark navy stripe running from the beak base across the eye and meeting at the chin to form the bib. Use the size-40 needle and small wisps; keep the edges clean.
Step 4: Make the beak and tail

Roll a tiny cone of dark roving for the beak, about 8 mm long. Felt it to a point on the mat, then stab it onto the front of the face, angling slightly downward. For the tail, tease a small flat piece of olive-green roving about 2 cm long, needle it flat and firm, then attach it at the rear of the body.

Roll two tiny black balls, about 3 mm each. Felt each one until very firm, then position them in the centre of each white cheek area. Stab from several angles to secure. A tiny white highlight dot on each eye lifts the expression.

items.problem: The colour zones bleed into each other
items.fix: Use thinner wisps and build colour in two or three passes. The size-40 needle gives more precision at boundaries.

items.cause: Beak not felted firmly enough before attachment
items.fix: Felt the beak more firmly before attaching, then use long deep strokes into the body to secure it.
